channel.title: MathematicalUniverse on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in MathematicalUniverse on emsenn.net

item.title: Semiotic Universe Agda Formalization
item.description:
Agda Formalization of the Semiotic Universe

Agda formalization of the semiotic universe specification, taking the synthetic approach: structures are built from categorical primitives rather than axiomatized.

Approach

The spec says: “We work inside a Grothendieck universe U.” In Agda, the universe hierarchy Set₀ : Set₁ : Set₂ : ... plays the role of the Grothendieck universe — Lean’s type-theoretic universes already have the closure properties (closed under dependent products, sums, etc.) that ZFC needs an explicit Grothendieck universe axiom to guarantee.

item.title: Semiotic Universe Formalization
item.description:
Formalization of the Semiotic Universe

This directory contains formal verifications of the semiotic universe specification in two proof assistants, each with a different foundational approach.

Directory structure

formalization/
├── lean/ Lean 4 + Mathlib — axiomatic approach
└── agda/ Agda — synthetic approach from Grothendieck universe

Two approaches, one spec

Both formalizations verify the same specification. They differ in how they relate to the mathematical foundations:

Lean (axiomatic)

The Lean formalization axiomatizes the algebraic consequences of the construction: H is postulated to be a Heyting algebra, j a Lawvere-Tierney topology, G a Heyting-comonadic trace. Properties like meet-preservation are axioms justified by the spec’s prose.

item.title: Semiotic Universe Lean Formalization
item.description:
Lean Formalization of the Semiotic Universe

Lean 4 + Mathlib formalization of the semiotic universe specification.

Approach

This formalization takes the axiomatic approach: it postulates that H is a Heyting algebra and that j and G satisfy certain properties, then proves that the derived lemmas follow. The axioms are justified by the constructive arguments in the spec (H = Sub(Y) in a topos, j is a Lawvere-Tierney topology, etc.), but the construction itself is not formalized here.

item.title: Formalizing the Semiotic Universe in Lean
item.description:
What this lesson covers

How to translate the formal specification of the semiotic universe into verified Lean 4 proofs. The specification has ten sections of mathematics. This lesson maps each section to a Lean formalization target: what typeclass or theorem it becomes, what Mathlib infrastructure it uses, and what needs to be proved.

The Lean formalization lives at ../formalization/. Step 1 (the ambient structure: H, j, G, and their interaction axioms) is implemented across four atomic files. This lesson maps the remaining spec sections to Lean formalization targets.

item.title: Dynamical Universe Specification
item.description:
Preface

This document specifies the Dynamical Universe: a mathematical universe whose primitive data and axioms are drawn from empirical processual phenomena. Unlike the Semiotic Universe and QCHTTopos, which validate against established mathematical theorem, the Dynamical Universe validates against empirical laws and experimental evidence. Its subject matter is process: how things move, evolve, and transform over time.

The specification is derived whole-cloth from empirical observation. The starting point is not an abstract mathematical structure but observable dynamical phenomena: thermodynamic processes, cosmological expansion, ecological succession, fluid dynamics, population dynamics, chemical kinetics. The question guiding the construction is: what is the minimal mathematical structure that faithfully represents these phenomena and can be validated against experimental time-series data?

item.title: Spectral Universe Specification
item.description:
Preface

This document specifies the Spectral Universe: a mathematical universe whose primitive data and axioms are drawn from empirical structural phenomena. Unlike the Semiotic Universe and QCHTTopos, which validate against established mathematical theorem, the Spectral Universe validates against empirical laws and experimental evidence. Its subject matter is structure: how measurement reveals discrete outcomes, how spectral decomposition exposes internal organization, and how the act of measurement interacts with what is measured.

The specification is derived whole-cloth from empirical observation. The starting point is not an abstract mathematical structure but observable structural phenomena: atomic spectral lines, crystallographic diffraction patterns, quantum measurement outcomes, nuclear magnetic resonance spectra, scattering cross-sections. The question guiding the construction is: what is the minimal mathematical structure that faithfully represents these phenomena and can be validated against spectral and structural data?

item.title: Core Semiotic Universe
item.description:
The interactive semioverse extends a semiotic universe — a self-sustaining sign system in which everything sayable is meaningful and everything meaningful is sayable. The base layer provides:

  • a meaning domain HH that is a complete Heyting algebra (intuitionistic, because not every question about a sign’s meaning resolves to yes or no),
  • a modal closure operator jj formalizing habit-formation,
  • a trace comonad GG formalizing semiotic provenance — the sign chains that produced each meaning,
  • definable operators interpreted into HH (the grammar of the sign system),
  • fragments (bounded interpretive contexts) and a composite closure operator S\mathcal{S}.

This layer ensures that all semantic work is closed under logic, habit-formation, and semiotic provenance before any handle-facing interaction is added.

item.title: Footprints, Delta, and Subtraction
item.description:
With interaction surfaces in place, we can define how a handle affects the meaning domain and how to compare or remove that influence. These three operations formalize the pragmatic dimension of semiosis: meanings have consequences (footprints), those consequences can be compared (delta), and influence can be disciplinedly removed (subtraction).

Footprint

The footprint of a handle is the closure of its handle fragment under the state’s operators — the total meaning the handle acquires through all its semiotic encounters. As a physical footprint indexes the passage of a body, the semiotic footprint indexes the passage of a handle through the sign system.

item.title: Fragments and Fusion
item.description:
What this lesson covers

How the semiotic universe achieves coherence between its syntax and semantics. Fragments are finite substructures that enable local reasoning; fusion is the closure process that identifies syntactic operators agreeing on all fragments and names new operators for behaviors already present in the system. The three closure operators — semantic, syntactic, and fusion — compose into a global operator whose least fixed point defines the semiotic universe itself.

Prerequisites

The Semantic Domain and Syntactic Operators. This lesson uses concepts from both.

item.title: Handles and Interactions
item.description:
The interactive semioverse introduces handles as indexical references and defines a calculus of interactions that generate semantic material — formalizing how signs engage with what lies outside the sign system.

Handles

A handle is a persistent indexical reference to a thing. It is not itself an element of the meaning domain HH; it is an external pointer mapped into HH by a seed function η\eta. This formalizes Peirce’s index: a sign connected to its object by existential relation rather than convention or resemblance.

item.title: Syntactic Operators
item.description:
What this lesson covers

The syntactic side of the semiotic universe: how a typed lambda calculus generates the compositional grammar of the sign system, what it means to interpret syntactic operators in the semantic domain, and what constraints the interpretation must satisfy.

Prerequisites

The Semantic Domain. Familiarity with the typed lambda calculus is helpful.

Types, terms, and raw operators

The semiotic universe’s syntax is generated by a typed lambda calculus. The type language Ty\mathsf{Ty} is built from:

item.title: The Semantic Domain
item.description:
What this lesson covers

The semantic side of the semiotic universe: the complete Heyting algebra HH that houses all meanings, the modal closure operator jj that distinguishes stable from provisional meanings, and the trace comonad GG that tracks how meanings unfold over time. These three structures — logical, modal, and temporal — and the axioms governing their interaction form the ambient environment in which everything else is built.

Prerequisites

Familiarity with Heyting algebras and closure operators. The lesson on Intuitionistic Logic provides useful background. Knowledge of comonads is helpful but not required — we explain the core idea here.

item.title: The Semiotic Universe
item.description:
What this lesson covers

The semiotic universe as a completed construction: what it is, what its universal property says, and why it is initial in a 2-category of semiotic structures. This lesson also connects the mathematical construction back to the semiotic ideas that motivate it — showing how the formal structure corresponds to Peirce’s theory of signs, semiosis, and interpretive practice.

Prerequisites

The Semantic Domain, Syntactic Operators, and Fragments and Fusion. The semiotics lessons on Signs and Interpretants and Semiosis and Sign Processes provide the semiotic background.

item.title: Generative fibered Recognition Trace Universe
item.description:
0. Introduction

This paper develops the mathematical foundations of a structure we call a
Generative Fibered Relational Trace Universe (GFRTU).
A GFRTU is a universe of semantic objects built from minimal relational data: a trace site, a family of fibered recognitions, and the dynamics of stabilization and drift.
Everything else—sheaf semantics, internal logic, local universes (cells), stability spaces, and higher layers of structure—is generated from these minimal commitments by three interacting closure principles.

item.title: Semiotic Universe
item.description:
Abstract

A sign system in which syntactic combination and semantic interpretation are mutually constrained — where every expressible form has a determinate meaning and every constructible meaning has a syntactic name — is a self-sustaining semiotic structure. This paper asks: what is the minimal such structure? The answer is a mathematical universe built within a Grothendieck universe UU, constructed as follows:

  • a meaning domain HH is constructed as the subobject lattice of a category of sign relations within UU, yielding a complete Heyting algebra — intuitionistic rather than classical, because not every question about a sign’s meaning resolves to yes or no. HH is equipped with:
    • a modal closure operator jj, formalizing habit-formation — the tendency of meanings to stabilize through repeated semiosis into habitual interpretations (Peirce’s ultimate logical interpretant), and
    • a trace comonad GG, formalizing the sign chain — the provenance that meanings carry from the semiosis that produced them (each interpretant is itself a sign, and the chain composes).
  • syntactic operators are generated by a typed lambda calculus, providing the grammar by which signs compose
  • a fusion mechanism interprets those syntactic operators as semantic endomorphisms, enforcing coherence between syntax and semantics — the formal counterpart of semiosis, the process by which syntactic forms acquire determinate meanings through their interpretive relations.

Three monotone, inflationary closure operators Ssem,Ssyn,Sfus\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{sem}}, \mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{syn}}, \mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{fus}} act on the complete lattice of partial structures. Their composite S\mathcal{S} has a least fixed point, which we define as the semiotic universe.
